quote: Mercy and truth have met; righteousness and peace have kissed.
firstReading: Amos promises the restoration of David’s fallen tent: God will rebuild what was broken and restore the land. The passage envisions returned people living securely—cities rebuilt, vineyards planted, and never again uprooted. It is a covenantal renewal: the Lord will plant Israel on its soil and establish lasting prosperity. The tone is hopeful and restorative, turning exile into permanent settlement and signaling divine faithfulness to promises made to the ancestors.
psalmSummary: Psalm 85 prays for God’s favorable presence and celebrates reconciliation between divine mercy and human renewal. It asks God to revive and save, then affirms that mercy and truth, righteousness and peace meet together. The psalm portrays salvation as communal blessing: God’s steadfast love brings flourishing and his justice heals the land. It anticipates healing and harmony when God again favors his people and restores their fortunes.
gospelSummary: In Matthew 9:14-17, Jesus answers questions about fasting by contrasting old forms with the new life he brings. When disciples of John and Pharisees ask why his followers do not fast, Jesus replies with wedding imagery: while the bridegroom is present, guests do not mourn. He then teaches that no one patches an old garment with new cloth nor puts new wine into old wineskins—new wine requires new, flexible containers. The lesson stresses that the kingdom’s newness cannot be confined by old structures; its life renews practices and requires adapted forms.
saintReflection: Saint Elizabeth of Portugal (Optional Memorial): Saint Elizabeth of Portugal was a queen whose public responsibilities became a field for charity, reconciliation, and care for the poor. She worked to bring peace within her family and kingdom, and after widowhood embraced Franciscan simplicity and prayer. Her life shows that holiness can sanctify influence when authority is used for mercy, peacemaking, and the protection of the vulnerable.
dailyPrayer: Lord of restoration and mercy, you who rebuild what is broken and bring joy out of exile, hear our prayer. Teach us to welcome your newness with humility, to set aside worn attitudes that choke your life, and to become vessels fit for your Spirit. Grant leaders the wisdom of Saint Elizabeth: to use authority for reconciliation, protection of the poor, and the pursuit of peace. Help us recognize when mercy and truth meet and to cooperate with you in healing families and communities. Strengthen our fasting of heart—our hunger for justice, charity, and conversion—so your kingdom grows among us. Amen.
theologicalSynthesis: The readings gather around God’s restorative promise and the need for forms flexible enough to receive divine renewal. Amos offers a prophetic vision of rebuilt life and settled prosperity; Psalm 85 celebrates mercy meeting truth and the peace that flows from God’s favor. Matthew warns that the coming of the Messiah brings a new dispensation—one that cannot be squeezed into old rituals or inflexible structures. Saint Elizabeth’s witness shows how authority can incarnate that mercy: by reconciling, protecting the vulnerable, and modeling simple service. Together the texts call the Church to accompany God’s restoration by renewing institutions, practices, and hearts so that mercy, righteousness, and peace may flourish in concrete ways.
exegesis:
Context: Amos 9:11-15 concludes a prophetic book largely directed at social injustice. Amos, a rural prophet, confronts exploitation and empty religiosity. This closing promise of restoration is striking: after judgment themes, God pledges to rebuild ‘‘the fallen tent of David’’ and to replant the land, signaling a return to covenantal fullness. The image of rebuilding David’s tent evokes a messianic hope—a renewed rule and secure settlement. It is less about dynastic politics than about God’s commitment to restore communal life and flourishing after catastrophe.

Psalm: Psalm 85 balances lament and hope. Opening as a prayer for God’s favor, it recalls past mercies before pleading for renewed salvation. Verse 10—‘‘Mercy and truth have met; righteousness and peace have kissed’’—offers a theological vision where divine attributes converge in salvation. The psalmist imagines reconciliation as a visible, relational reality: God’s steadfast love and faithfulness produce righteousness and peace among a people whose land and life are healed. The language ties personal repentance to communal restoration, reflecting Amos’s promise of a transformed homeland.

Gospel: Matthew 9:14-17 places Jesus’ ministry within religious practice debates. Questions about fasting index expectations for a prophetic successor like John. Jesus’ responses refuse simple ritual conformity. The bridegroom image locates joy in presence rather than obligation. The metaphors of patching and wineskins teach about incompatibility between new and old: a new revelation needs new forms. The new wine is the kingdom’s vitality; old wineskins are rigid institutions unable to contain transforming grace. Matthew invites discernment about which traditions preserve life and which must be renewed or left behind.

Saints: Saint Elizabeth of Portugal lives out a Christian synthesis of power and penitential simplicity. As queen she enacted mercy and peacemaking—reconciling families and defending the poor—thus giving political authority a sacramental shape. After widowhood she adopted Franciscan poverty, showing conversion from courtly power to prophetic witness. Her life models how ecclesial and civic roles can be reformed so that mercy and truth meet practically in governance: institutions designed for rule can become instruments of reconciliation and social repair.

Today: The challenge for contemporary believers is concrete: how to make structures hospitable to the new wine of the Gospel. Amos warns against complacent wealth and injustice; the psalm yearns for God to heal the land; Matthew insists that the kingdom’s arrival requires adaptive forms. Pastoral leaders and lay faithful alike must ask where traditions preserve the Gospel’s life and where they have calcified. Like Elizabeth, Christians in positions of influence are called to use power to protect the vulnerable and broker peace. The season of restoration calls for charity that is systemic as well as personal.

Conclusion: Together the readings propose a theology of conversion that is communal, institutional, and spiritual. God’s promise of rebuilding invites participation: communities must be reshaped so mercy, truth, righteousness, and peace can meet. The Gospel’s metaphoric teaching cautions against nostalgia that preserves structures at the expense of life, while the example of Saint Elizabeth shows how leadership committed to mercy can incarnate renewed forms of Christian charity. The faithful are invited to fast from habits that hinder justice, to fashion new wineskins—flexible, compassionate institutions—and to labor for the healing of families, churches, and nations.
